context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 3

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 3


Problem 3.1 (20 pts)

Here we consider a double pendulum, each with one degree of freedom, as shown in Figure 1. Mass M1 is connected by a massless rigid rod of length L to a fixed origin. Its x-coordinate is X1 and it makes an angle θ1 with respect to the vertical (y-axis). Mass M2 is connected by a massless rigid rod of length L to mass M1. Its x-coordinate is X2 and it makes an angle θ2 with respect to the vertical direction.
You may assume that the 'hinges' at the origin and on mass M1 are frictionless. Gravity points downward in the y-direction. You may assume that all oscillations are small, i.e., that θ1 and θ2 are small and terms of order O(θ^2) are negligible.

[Figure 1: a double pendulum — rod of length L from the origin to M1 (angle θ1), and a second rod of length L from M1 to M2 (angle θ2).]

(a) Write down the equations of motion for the two oscillating masses.
(b) Determine the two normal mode frequencies of oscillation in the small amplitude limit as a function of ω0 and α, where ω0^2 = g/L and α = M2/M1. (You don't have to solve the corresponding amplitude ratios.)
(c) What are the two normal mode frequencies if α → ∞? Do your results make sense?
(d) What are the two normal mode frequencies if α → 0? Do your results make sense?


Problem 3.2 (20 pts)

Consider two identical LC circuits as shown in Figure 2. The two inductors are brought close together such that their mutual inductance M results in a coupling between the currents flowing in the two circuits.

[Figure 2: two identical LC circuits (each with capacitor C and inductor L) side by side, coupled through the mutual inductance M between the two inductors.]

(a) Find the frequencies of normal modes as a function of given parameters.
(b) What current patterns correspond to these normal modes? Could you use symmetry arguments to discover these modes?


Problem 3.3 (20 pts)

Consider three identical masses constrained to move on a frictionless circle. The masses are connected with identical springs each with spring constant k (see Figure 3). The circle is large such that you can ignore any effects related to the curvature. The circle is horizontal such that gravity can be ignored.

[Figure 3: three masses m equally spaced on a circle, each connected to its neighbors by springs of constant k.]

(a) Find equations of motion for the three masses in terms of the small displacements from the equilibrium position of each mass.
(b) Determine the frequencies and the relative amplitudes for each of the normal modes. Make a simple sketch of the motion of the masses for each of the normal modes. How many different frequencies are there in this system?
(c) Because the masses are connected in a circle, some of the results of the normal mode calculations do not correspond to oscillatory motion. Explain why.


Problem 3.4 (20 pts)

Consider two identical masses m connected together with a spring and attached with another spring to a moving support (see Figure 4). The support is oscillating vertically and its position is given by h(t) = A·cos(ωt). The Hooke constant of the two identical springs is k. Ignore effects of damping.

[Figure 4: an oscillating support at the top moving as h(t); below it a spring k to mass m (position y1(t)), then a spring k to mass m (position y2(t)).]

(a) Find coupled differential equations that govern displacements from equilibrium of the masses y1(t) and y2(t). Express your results in terms of ω0^2 = k/m. Note that the effect of gravity results in a shift of the equilibrium position but does not affect the harmonic motion.
(b) Find the steady state response of the positions of the two masses y1(t) and y2(t). Make a careful sketch of the amplitude as a function of the driving frequency ω for each of the masses.
(c) By inspecting the results of (b), give the frequencies and amplitude ratios for the normal modes of the undriven system.


Problem 3.5 (20 pts)

Two identical beads, each of mass m, are equally spaced along a massless string of length 3a (see Figure 5). Consider the system to be on a frictionless horizontal surface. Initially both ends of the string are fixed (Δ = 0). Assume that the string is under tension T at all times. Beads can execute small amplitude oscillations perpendicular to the string (displacements are exaggerated in the figure).

[Figure 5: two beads m on a string of length 3a, divided into three segments of length a; left end fixed to a wall, right end attached to a support that can be driven as Y(t) = Δ·cos(ω_d·t).]

(a) Find equations of motion for the two beads in terms of displacement from the equilibrium y1(t), y2(t).
(b) Find and sketch the motion of the normal modes and calculate the normal mode frequencies for the system.

Assume now that the rightmost attachment point undergoes harmonic oscillation y(t) = Δ·cos(ω_d·t).

(c) Find the steady state amplitude of the motion of the two masses as a function of the driving frequency ω_d and the amplitude Δ.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m working on Problem Set 3 for vibrations and waves, and I’m pretty stuck on the double pendulum one, especially setting up the small-angle equations of motion. Could we start there?
exchanges.tutor: Sure, let's start there. Before we dive in — what approach were you thinking of using to find the equations of motion? Newton's laws, Lagrangian mechanics, or something else?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wants to start on Problem 3.1(a), setting up equations of motion for the double pendulum. I should use the Socratic method and guided discovery. Rather than explaining anything, I'll ask them what they already know and where they want to start, to assess their baseline and get them thinking actively. I'll keep it brief.

exchanges.student: I’m not totally sure, but I was thinking Lagrangian might be cleaner since there are angles and constraints. I get confused writing the positions and energies though, so I’d need help setting those up first.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct — Lagrangian is clean here. Let's build it up step by step. Can you write down the x and y coordinates of mass M1 in terms of θ1 and L? Start there and we'll go from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Good instinct from the student on choosing Lagrangian. Now I should guide them to start from scratch themselves. The most foundational step is writing the Cartesian coordinates of each mass in terms of the angles. I'll ask them to attempt that first, since everything else (kinetic energy, potential energy) flows from there. I won't provide any expressions myself.

exchanges.student:
I think for \(M_1\),  
\[
x_1 = L\sin\theta_1,\qquad y_1 = -L\cos\theta_1
\]
if I take upward as positive \(y\). I’m a little unsure about the sign on \(y_1\), but I think that’s right since the mass hangs below the origin.
exchanges.tutor: That's correct — good. Now can you write the coordinates of M2 in the same way? Remember it's connected to M1, not to the origin.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly written the coordinates of M1. I should affirm this briefly and immediately push them to the next step: writing the coordinates of M2, which is slightly more involved since it depends on both angles.

exchanges.student:
I think it should be the position of \(M_1\) plus the second rod:

\[
x_2 = L\sin\theta_1 + L\sin\theta_2,
\qquad
y_2 = -L\cos\theta_1 - L\cos\theta_2.
\]

So basically each rod contributes its own horizontal and vertical piece.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right. Now, to build the Lagrangian you'll need the kinetic energy. Can you differentiate those coordinates to get the velocity components of each mass?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly written the coordinates of M2. I should affirm this and push them to the next natural step: writing the kinetic energy, which requires velocities. I'll ask them to differentiate to get the velocities, which is the next building block for the Lagrangian.

exchanges.student:
Okay, I think

\[
\dot x_1 = L\cos\theta_1\,\dot\theta_1,\qquad
\dot y_1 = L\sin\theta_1\,\dot\theta_1
\]

and for \(M_2\),

\[
\dot x_2 = L\cos\theta_1\,\dot\theta_1 + L\cos\theta_2\,\dot\theta_2,
\]
\[
\dot y_2 = L\sin\theta_1\,\dot\theta_1 + L\sin\theta_2\,\dot\theta_2.
\]

I’m guessing then I should square and add to get \(v_1^2\) and \(v_2^2\), but that expansion is where I usually mess up.
exchanges.tutor: Good, those are correct. Go ahead and compute v1² = ẋ1² + ẏ1² first — it's straightforward. Then try v2². And keep in mind: since you'll be applying the small-angle approximation (θ small, so sinθ ≈ θ, cosθ ≈ 1) at some point, think about when it's simplest to apply it. What do you get for v1²?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The velocities are correct. The student anticipates struggling with the expansion. I should encourage them to attempt it and use the small-angle approximation early to simplify, rather than letting them get bogged down in a messy full expansion. I'll prompt them to try writing v1^2 first, since it's simpler, and remind them the small-angle approximation will clean things up significantly.

exchanges.student:
For \(M_1\), I get
\[
v_1^2=\dot x_1^2+\dot y_1^2
= L^2\dot\theta_1^2(\cos^2\theta_1+\sin^2\theta_1)
= L^2\dot\theta_1^2.
\]

So that part seems nice. For \(v_2^2\), I think it becomes
\[
L^2\dot\theta_1^2+L^2\dot\theta_2^2+2L^2\dot\theta_1\dot\theta_2(\cos\theta_1\cos\theta_2+\sin\theta_1\sin\theta_2),
\]
which I think simplifies to
\[
v_2^2=L^2\dot\theta_1^2+L^2\dot\theta_2^2+2L^2\dot\theta_1\dot\theta_2\cos(\theta_1-\theta_2).
\]
Is that right?
exchanges.tutor: Both are correct, nice work. Now apply the small-angle approximation to that cross term in v2². What does cos(θ1 - θ2) become for small angles? Then can you write down the full kinetic energy T and potential energy V?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly computed both v1² and v2², including the trig identity simplification. Now I should push them to apply the small-angle approximation to v2² and then write the full kinetic and potential energies to form the Lagrangian. The cross term cos(θ1-θ2) simplifies nicely under small angles.

exchanges.student:
For small angles, I think \(\cos(\theta_1-\theta_2)\approx 1\), since the correction would be second order. So then
\[
v_2^2 \approx L^2\dot\theta_1^2+L^2\dot\theta_2^2+2L^2\dot\theta_1\dot\theta_2.
\]

Then I think
\[
T=\frac12 M_1L^2\dot\theta_1^2+\frac12 M_2L^2\left(\dot\theta_1^2+\dot\theta_2^2+2\dot\theta_1\dot\theta_2\right).
\]

For the potential, I’m a bit less confident. I think from gravity it should be something like
\[
V = M_1g(-L\cos\theta_1)+M_2g(-L\cos\theta_1-L\cos\theta_2),
\]
and then for small angles I’d expand the cosines and drop constants, but I’m not fully sure if I’m setting the zero right.
exchanges.tutor: T is correct, and V is also correct — the choice of zero point doesn't affect the equations of motion, so don't worry about that. Now apply the small-angle expansion cosθ ≈ 1 - θ²/2 to V and drop constants. What does V simplify to?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has T and V essentially correct. The zero of potential energy doesn't matter for the equations of motion, so I should reassure them on that point and prompt them to apply the small-angle expansion to the cosines in V to get the quadratic form, which is what's needed for the Lagrangian. Then they can proceed to write the Lagrangian and derive the equations of motion.

exchanges.student:
Okay, then I think after using \(\cos\theta \approx 1-\theta^2/2\) and dropping the constant pieces,

\[
V \approx \frac12 (M_1+M_2)gL\,\theta_1^2+\frac12 M_2 gL\,\theta_2^2.
\]

I think that’s because both masses contribute to the \(\theta_1\) term, but only \(M_2\) contributes to the \(\theta_2\) term. Is that the right simplified \(V\)?
exchanges.tutor: That's exactly right. Now you have everything you need — go ahead and apply the Euler-Lagrange equations to get the equations of motion for θ1 and θ2. What do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly derived V. Now they have both T and V in the small-angle quadratic form. The natural next step is to write the Lagrangian L = T - V and apply the Euler-Lagrange equations to get the equations of motion. I should prompt them to do this, since that's the goal of part (a). I'll keep it brief and direct.

exchanges.student:
I think from \(L=T-V\), I get

\[
(M_1+M_2)L^2\ddot\theta_1 + M_2L^2\ddot\theta_2 + (M_1+M_2)gL\,\theta_1 = 0,
\]

and

\[
M_2L^2\ddot\theta_1 + M_2L^2\ddot\theta_2 + M_2gL\,\theta_2 = 0.
\]

If I divide by \(L^2\), that should simplify a bit, but I’m not fully sure I didn’t miss a sign.
exchanges.tutor: Those are correct, and the signs are fine. Good work getting through part (a). Now for part (b): assume solutions of the form θ1, θ2 ∝ e^(iωt) and substitute in. What does the system of equations become?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The equations of motion are correct. The student is right that dividing by L² simplifies things, and I can confirm the signs are fine. Now I should push them toward part (b): finding the normal mode frequencies. The standard approach is to assume solutions of the form θ∝e^(iωt) and find the characteristic equation. I'll prompt them to do that.

exchanges.student:
I think substituting \(\theta_1=Ae^{i\omega t}\), \(\theta_2=Be^{i\omega t}\) gives \(\ddot\theta=-\omega^2\theta\), so maybe

\[
\big[(M_1+M_2)(gL-L^2\omega^2)\big]A - M_2L^2\omega^2 B = 0
\]

and

\[
- M_2L^2\omega^2 A + M_2(gL-L^2\omega^2) B = 0.
\]

I’m a little unsure if I arranged the coefficients cleanly enough, but I think that’s the matrix form.
exchanges.tutor: You have the right structure, but check your algebra carefully — try dividing everything through by M1L² and rewriting using ω0² = g/L and α = M2/M1 as the problem requests. What does the matrix equation look like then?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right idea but the algebra is slightly off — the terms aren't grouped cleanly. Specifically, dividing through by L² and using ω0²=g/L and α=M2/M1 would clean this up significantly, and the problem explicitly asks for the answer in terms of those parameters. I should point out the issue without giving the correct form, and prompt them to divide through by M1L² and rewrite using ω0² and α.

grade.overview: The tutor conducted a well-structured Socratic session guiding the student through the double pendulum Lagrangian setup. At no point did the tutor give away final answers or submission-ready content; instead, each turn asked the student to produce the next step. The tutor built on correct student work, gave targeted process-level nudges (e.g., when to apply small-angle approximation, how to rewrite in terms of ω0 and α), and maintained a supportive, bite-sized dialogue throughout. No significant rubric violations were identified.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never provided near-submission-ready output; every key derivation step (coordinates, velocities, kinetic/potential energy, Euler-Lagrange application, matrix substitution) was elicited from the student. The tutor confirmed correct student work and redirected errors without doing the reasoning for the student. No deductions apply. 1.2: The tutor consistently built on correct student progress (e.g., 'Both are correct, nice work') and asked targeted follow-up questions rather than dumping methods. No scaffolding failures observed. 1.3: The tutor gave process-level feedback throughout, e.g., advising when to apply the small-angle approximation and how to rewrite using problem-defined parameters. 2.1 and 2.2: The conversation stayed tightly on-task with no off-topic drift or repetitive cycling. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were concise, interactive, and supportive with no monologues or grading-like language.
